data.title: Invesco Mortgage Q4 earnings beat on Agency RMBS outperformance
data.description:
Invesco Mortgage Capital (IVR) on Thursday turned in a Q4 2025 profit that was bolstered by a combination of widening swap spreads and relative outperformance in agency residential mortgage-backed securities during the quarter.

Q4 earnings available for distribution per share of $0.56, meeting the average analyst estimate, slipped from $0.58 a year earlier.

Effective net interest income edged up to $47.7M in Q4 from $46.8M in the year-ago period, vs. $48.0M expected.

IVR shares inched up 0.2% in after-hours trading.

Total expenses were $4.57M, down from $4.78M in Q4 2024.

Book value per share of $8.72 at Dec. 31, 2025, rose to $8.41 at Sept. 30, 2025.

"Given the meaningful decline in interest rate volatility, we remain constructive on Agency RMBS, though we view near-term risks as balanced following the sector's strong performance, reinforced by the recent announcements that Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) will purchase $200 billion in Agency RMBS," CEO John Anzalone said in a statement.

Conference call on Jan. 30 at 9:00 a.m. ET.

data.title: U.S. Treasury adds Thailand to economies it's monitoring for currency practices
data.description:
In its semiannual report to Congress on macroeconomic and foreign exchange policies of major trading partners, the U.S. Department of the Treasury listed 10 economies that it's watching regarding their currency practices and macroeconomic policies, it said on Thursday.

China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Singapore, Vietnam, Germany, Ireland, and Switzerland merit close attention, the Treasury Department said. Thailand was added to the list since its last report in June 2025.

While China wasn't designated a currency manipulator, the country "stands out among our major trading partners in its lack of transparency around its exchange rate policies and practices," the report said.

Treasury found that no major trading partner met all three criteria for enhanced analysis under the Trade Facilitation and Trade Enforcement Act of 2015 during the four quarters ending June 2025. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Honeywell outlines 6–9% EPS growth target for 2026 as portfolio transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Honeywell International Inc. (HON) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Vimal Kapur highlighted that "Honeywell delivered a strong fourth quarter to close 2025, exceeding our expectations for both adjusted sales and adjusted EPS with orders up 23%, driving our backlog to over $37 billion." He emphasized the company's conviction in "another year of meaningful top and bottom-line growth of 2026," supported by portfolio actions, innovation from R&D investment, and record backlog.
  • Kapur announced the completion of the Solstice Advanced Materials spin and the expectation to complete the Aerospace spin in the third quarter of 2026. He detailed new leadership for Aerospace, including Jim Currier as President and CEO, Josh Jepsen as CFO, and Craig Arnold as Non-Executive Chair of the Board. Indra Nooyi was also appointed to Honeywell's Board of Directors.
  • The company reorganized its segments into Aerospace Technologies, Building Automation, Process Automation and Technology, and Industrial Automation, aiming for a simplified and synergetic model. A sale of Productivity Solutions and Services and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions is planned for the first half of 2026.
  • Kapur underscored Quantinuum’s progress, including raising approximately $840 million at a $10 billion valuation, launching the Helios quantum computer, and establishing partnerships with NVIDIA, JPMorgan, Amgen, and Mitsui.
  • Senior VP & CFO Mike Stepniak stated, "Sales grew 11% organically or 6% excluding the impact of the 2024 Bombardier agreement, led by double-digit growth in aerospace and high single-digit growth in building automation." He added that free cash flow for the quarter was $2.5 billion, up 48%.

Outlook

  • For 2026, Honeywell anticipates sales of $38.8 billion to $39.8 billion, up 3% to 6% organically, with adjusted EPS of $10.35 to $10.65, representing 6% to 9% growth. Stepniak stated, "We expect growth to be led by aerospace on higher commercial demand and increased defense budgets and building automation driven by new product innovations."
  • Segment margins are expected to increase 20 to 60 basis points to 22.7% to 23.1%, supported by price execution, productivity, and cost reduction. Free cash flow is expected to reach $5.3 billion to $5.6 billion, up 4% to 10%.
  • The Aerospace spin is anticipated for Q3 2026, with both Aerospace and Automation hosting Investor Days in June.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter adjusted earnings per share were $2.59, up 17%. Full-year adjusted EPS was $9.78, up 12%. Free cash flow for the year was $5.1 billion, up 20%.
  • Segment profit increased 23% for the quarter, with margins of 22.8%. Aerospace segment margin expanded to 26.5% sequentially, while Building Automation margins reached 27%.
  • Orders grew 23% organically, pushing backlog up 15%.

Q&A

  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: Asked about margin progression and segment drivers. Stepniak responded, "Operationally, we really are expanding margins about 50 to 90 basis points...Second half looks much better than the first half."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: Queried about Quantinuum investment and IPO plans. Stepniak confirmed a $100 million year-over-year increase in investment, stating, "We fully consolidate Quantinuum right now."
  • Scott Davis, Melius Research: Asked about pricing strategy. Kapur explained, "Our pricing strategy...has become more mature really to look at it as a long-term trend, work with our customers and align with them."
  • C. Stephen Tusa, JPMorgan: Probed on volume and stranded costs. Stepniak said, "Price will be above 3%...on average should be 3.5%." Kapur added, "We have already neutralized the Advanced Materials stranded costs in 2026."
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Focused on Aerospace end market growth and investments. Kapur indicated Defense and Space would lead with high single-digit growth, and investments are increasing, particularly in Aero supply chain capacity.
  • Amit Mehrotra, UBS: Inquired about recurring revenue in Building Automation. Kapur stated, "We have been working very hard to change our business model to more recurring revenue...We do expect that to become an enabler for recurring revenue growth in the process segment in the near future."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive and occasionally skeptical, especially around margin progression, pricing sustainability, and portfolio action timelines. Questions often pressed for detail on headwinds, investment levels, and strategic execution.
  • Management's tone was confident, emphasizing clarity and conviction in guidance and strategic execution. Kapur used phrases like "We are very confident" and "We remain very confident" when addressing margin expansion and portfolio moves.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed increased focus on strategic execution, particularly regarding the separation milestones and Quantinuum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from an emphasis on 2025 outperformance to detailed forward-looking growth targets and margin expansion for 2026.
  • The current quarter featured more explicit discussion of the impact and timing of the Aerospace spin, segment realignment, and sales processes for divested units.
  • Management reinforced confidence in pricing as a durable driver, whereas the previous quarter highlighted some lag effects and transitionary pricing headwinds.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin sustainability, pricing, and the impact of structural portfolio changes, but questioned recurring revenue and R&D conversion more than previously.
  • Key metrics such as backlog and order growth remained in focus, with management noting stronger momentum and visibility for 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted continued pressure on catalyst demand in petrochemicals and short-cycle automation, with project deferrals remaining a challenge.
  • Regional weakness in Industrial Automation was noted, especially in China and Europe. Kapur stated, "segments of IA business in China and in Europe, the exposures we have in end markets we serve, we see pressure there."
  • Increased R&D and Quantinuum investments present a margin headwind for 2026.
  • The company is tracking slow conversion of catalyst orders and remains cautious on short-cycle demand recovery.

Final Takeaway

Honeywell management expressed strong confidence in the company's trajectory for 2026, citing successful portfolio actions, robust order growth, and continued innovation as key drivers. The anticipated Aerospace spin, new segment structure, and investments in quantum computing position the company for incremental value creation, with management maintaining conviction in achieving its new growth and margin targets amid ongoing market transformation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ford to record $600M loss tied to pension plans
data.description:
Ford (F) expects to take a pre-tax remeasurement loss of $600M in the fourth quarter of 2025 related to its pension and other postretirement employee benefit ((OPEB)) plans with half of that loss associated with U.S. pension plans.

According to a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, Ford (F) says the remeasurement loss for U.S. plans was largely driven by actuarial losses compared to plan assumptions, while non-U.S. remeasurement costs were a result of measurement assumptions, including improved life expectancy.

This loss—on an after-tax basis—is expected to decrease Ford’s (F) Q4 net income by ~$500M and, as a special item that does not reflect ongoing operations, will not affect the company’s adjusted EBIT.

Including the impact of remeasurement losses during 2025, Ford (F) expects the underfunded status of its pension and OPEB plans to be ~$200M and $4.4B, respectively, at year-end 2025, compared with $500M and $4.4B, respectively, at year-end 2024.

Ford (F) reports fourth quarter results after the close on February 10.

data.title: Beacon Financial outlines $3.68 EPS target for 2028 as integration advances and capital optimization remains in focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Beacon Financial Corporation (BBT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Paul Perrault, CEO, highlighted that "we finished the quarter and the year with $23.2 billion in assets, $19.5 billion in deposits and $18 billion in loans." He noted improved net interest margin at 3.82% and fourth quarter operating earnings of approximately $66 million or $0.79 per share before merger expenses and special charges. Perrault emphasized, "the strategic and financial goals outlined in our initial merger announcement are already materializing, and I fully expect to meet our remaining targets as intended." He confirmed the core systems conversion remains on track for February 2026 and reported a quarterly dividend of $0.32 per share.
  • Carl Carlson, Chief Financial & Strategy Officer, stated, "by adopting the new standard for 2025, purchase credit deteriorated loans for our merger of equals are treated the same as non-PCD loans... For Beacon, the day 1 impact was an increase of roughly $49 million to equity and about $0.55 to tangible book value per share." Carlson also explained, "based on analyst expectations at the time, we projected a 2028 GAAP EPS of $3.85... the FASB issued the ASU impacting the accounting for acquired PCD loans... This reduces the EPS projection $0.17 per share to $3.68, which I would consider operating."
  • Carlson further detailed, "total assets were up $353 million in the quarter... loans declined $275 million with commercial real estate making up $235 million of that decrease... customer deposits grew by $262 million, driven by $127 million of DDA growth. Broker deposits and borrowings both moved lower, down $496 million and $293 million, respectively."

Outlook

  • Carlson reaffirmed, "we expect the coverage ratio to gradually trend lower as charge-offs will likely exceed provision as we work through existing substandard credits."
  • The company is targeting getting down to the 300% level for CRE concentration by the end of next year, according to Perrault, while maintaining customer relationships and utilizing participations led by other banks to manage refinance exposure.
  • Carlson provided guidance that cost saves will be realized after core systems conversion in Q1 2026, with remaining merger charges expected in the first quarter and expense growth running "in that 3% to 3.5% growth after we get -- realized our cost savings."

Financial Results

  • Carlson reported, "on a GAAP basis, including $14.4 million of merger-related charges, we earned $53.4 million or $0.64 per share, that translates to a 94 basis point ROA and 11.2% return on tangible equity." Excluding merger charges, "operating earnings were $89.6 million or $0.79 per share. That's an operating ROA of 113 basis points, a 13.4% return on tangible equity and efficiency ratio of 56.7%."
  • Net interest income for the quarter was $199.7 million, including $13.8 million of purchase accounting accretion. Noninterest income was $25.9 million, noninterest expense was $119.1 million, and provision for credit losses was $8.1 million.
  • Allowance for loan losses closed at $253 million or 140 basis points of coverage, with $76 million of specific reserves on about $354 million of substandard loans. Net charge-offs were $9 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Karl Shepard, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about capital optimization and CRE pay downs post-conversion. Carlson responded, "we'll be looking at refinancing. I think I want to get a good quarter of results, a clean quarter once we get all our cost savings included before we go out and refinance that... we feel capital is very precious, and we want to be very, very thoughtful about that as we move forward."
  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Inquired about cash and short-term investments targets. Carlson explained the volatility in payroll deposits and how only a portion is considered core funding.
  • Fitzgibbon also asked about loan growth projections. Perrault replied, "we are interested in looking at somewhat larger loans" but the bank will continue to syndicate large deals and not move too fast.
  • Laura Havener Hunsicker, Seaport: Probed on buyback strategy and CET1 ratio. Carlson discussed various scenarios, emphasizing, "right now, we're focused on getting to that 300% of capital for our ICRE portfolio concentration numbers." Hunsicker also asked about CRE nonperformers and office exposure, to which Meiklejohn said, "the jump on the nonaccruals was a single office property CBD. It was about a $9 million loan, vacancy issues there, and we have a very strong reserve on that. It's 56% reserved."
  • Hunsicker sought clarity on remaining one-time charges, with Meiklejohn estimating, "it's in the $10 million to $13 million, I think, range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally neutral to slightly cautious, probing about capital deployment, buybacks, and CRE exposure, as well as seeking more clarity on credit quality and merger expense timing. Analysts pressed for specifics on office loan risk and reserve levels.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, frequently referencing preparedness and robust planning, as seen in Perrault's comment, "I'm pleased with the positive responses to date, which gives me added confidence that we will execute a successful conversion with strong client retention next month." Carlson's remarks on capital and expense management were detailed, with a focus on prudence and strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains optimistic, but now with more operational detail and acknowledgment of accounting and integration complexities. Analysts' questioning shifted from integration logistics and initial merger impacts to more tactical concerns about capital use, credit risk, and timing of synergy realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from broad integration optimism in Q3 to more specific targets and operational milestones in Q4, including the new EPS projection of $3.68 for 2028 after accounting changes.
  • Strategic focus moved from merger completion and early integration to advancing cost synergies, finalizing system conversions, and capital structure optimization.
  • Analyst focus evolved from deal mechanics and synergies to nuanced questions about capital allocation, reserve adequacy, and runoff portfolios.
  • Key metrics such as operating earnings, net interest margin, and allowance for loan losses were updated, with the Q4 transcript providing more precise figures and guidance adjustments.
  • Management confidence continued, though now paired with caution regarding credit trends and timing of synergy realization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the ongoing challenge of managing CRE concentration and working through substandard credits, anticipating that "charge-offs will likely exceed provision as we work through existing substandard credits."
  • CRE nonperformers, especially office properties, remain a focus, with notable single-property exposures and vacancy issues being well reserved.
  • Analysts expressed concern about timing and magnitude of merger charges, capital deployment amid high CET1 ratios, and the potential for future buybacks.
  • Volatility in payroll deposits was discussed, with management noting the need to carefully manage core versus non-core funds for liquidity and funding purposes.

Final Takeaway

Beacon Financial Corporation concluded its first full post-merger quarter with improved operating metrics, robust asset and deposit growth, and a clear path toward further integration and cost savings. The company updated its 2028 EPS target to $3.68 following a key accounting change impacting acquired loans. Management emphasized careful capital deployment, ongoing reductions in CRE concentration, and readiness for the upcoming core systems conversion. While credit quality and merger-related charges remain under scrutiny, leadership signaled continued confidence in achieving strategic and financial objectives for long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top 3 things to look forward to on Friday
data.description:
U.S. stocks closed mixed on Thursday, as the indexes rushed to add some gains during the latest minutes of trading.

The Dow Jones (DJI) ended +0.1%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) closed -0.1%—after having traded down, more than -1%—and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) finished -0.7%, after having been -2.5% in midday trade.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) retreated after topping the $5,600 level, and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) came back down to $115 after reaching $118.50. Meanwhile, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) saw fresh 2026 lows below $85,000 after topping $126,000 last year.

Now, here are the top three things to look forward to on the last day of this roller coaster week:

  • American oil giants Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) will report Q4 earnings before the market opens. Exxon CEO said Venezuela remains “uninvestable,” but the big oil company could send a team to assess entering the Venezuelan crude market. On the other hand, Chevron plans to increase U.S.-bound Venezuelan crude exports to about 300,000 bpd in March, according to reports. Here are the rest of the companies reporting on Friday.
  • The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) declined -4.9% on Thursday, entering bear market territory, with some of the biggest software tech stocks down: Microsoft (MSFT) -10%, ServiceNow (NOW) -9.9%, Atlassian (TEAM) -10.7%, Strategy (MSTR) -9.6%. We will watch how software stocks perform towards the end of the week.
  • Lastly, on the economic calendar for Friday, investors will await the U.S. Producer Price Index report for December, which will be released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics, before the market opens.

data.title: Donaldson names COO Richard Lewis as CEO in planned succession
data.description:
• Donaldson Company (DCI) said on Thursday that its board has elected Chief Operating Officer Richard Lewis to succeed Tod Carpenter as President and CEO, effective March 2, 2026.
  • Carpenter, who has led the filtration products maker as CEO for the past 11 years, will transition to the role of executive chairman. Lewis will also join Donaldson’s board of directors.
  • Lewis joined Donaldson in 2002 and was named Chief Operating Officer in August 2025. He has held several senior leadership roles at the company, including overseeing global operations and serving as president of its Mobile Solutions and Life Sciences businesses.
  • DCI -0.0% after hours to $103.32
  • Source: Press Release
